stages.name: Discovery
stages.description: Uncover the customer's current browser environment, legacy app dependencies, security posture, and device landscape before pitching.

questions.question: How are your employees' browsers currently managed — does IT push policies centrally, or are users running whatever they like?
questions.purpose: Determine whether there's any existing browser governance — the starting point for the CEP conversation. Most orgs have zero visibility here.
followUps: Who in IT owns browser policy today?
Are browser updates handled centrally or left to individual users?
Do you have visibility into which extensions employees have installed?

industryTips.financial-services: Unmanaged browsers are a frequent finding in FCA and PCI-DSS audits — frame browser governance as a compliance gap, not just an IT preference.
industryTips.technology: Even tech-savvy teams often have blind spots here — browser-based credential harvesting and OAuth token theft target developers specifically.

answerChips.label: No central browser policy
answerChips.pitchBullet: Chrome Admin Console pushes security policies to every managed browser instantly — no user prompt or action required
answerChips.sayThis: So today, any employee can change their browser settings or disable protections without IT knowing. That's the exact gap CEP closes.

deepDive.howItWorks: Chrome Admin Console is a cloud-hosted management console — no on-prem infrastructure required. Connect it to Google Workspace or Entra ID/Azure AD, and every policy you configure applies the instant a user signs into managed Chrome with their work account: extension controls, DLP rules, safe browsing enforcement, site restrictions. The managed profile follows the user to any device they sign into. Set it once; it applies everywhere across the fleet.
additionalProtections: Policy sync in under 15 minutes — changes apply fleet-wide without any endpoint visits
Separate work and personal Chrome profiles on the same device — policies only apply inside the work context, personal browsing stays private
Force-install approved extensions and block high-risk ones from one central console
deepDive.proofPoint: Most organisations complete initial CEP policy configuration in 2–4 hours of IT time. Zero endpoint visits, zero user disruption.

answerChips.label: No extension visibility/control
answerChips.pitchBullet: CEP shows every extension installed across the entire fleet in real time, and lets IT block risky ones or force-install approved ones centrally
answerChips.sayThis: Browser extensions can read every page a user opens — including login screens and sensitive documents. Without visibility, you have no idea what's running across your org.

deepDive.howItWorks: The Chrome Admin Console provides a live inventory of every extension installed across your managed fleet — grouped by extension, user, or device. Each extension shows its name, version, publisher, permissions requested (e.g., 'access all site data'), and a risk classification. You can block specific extensions by ID, block entire categories (e.g., all screen recorders), force-install approved extensions, or set allowlist mode so users can only install pre-approved extensions. Changes apply within 15 minutes with no user action required.
additionalProtections: New extension install alerts — notified immediately when any user installs an extension not on your approved list
Extension update monitoring — flag when an approved extension receives a major update that changes its permission scope
Shadow IT discovery — extensions often reveal unsanctioned SaaS tools accessing sensitive pages (Grammarly reading HR docs, personal AI assistants reading CRM data)
deepDive.proofPoint: In a typical 500-seat first extension audit, organisations find an average of 12–15 high-risk extensions — screen recorders, in-browser credential managers, and productivity tools with excessive data access permissions.

answerChips.label: Users self-update (or don't)
answerChips.pitchBullet: CEP enforces silent Chrome auto-updates across all managed browsers — always current, no user prompt, no exceptions
answerChips.sayThis: Outdated browsers are one of the top five exploit entry points. If updates depend on users, you'll always have a tail of vulnerable versions.

deepDive.howItWorks: CEP's update policy enforces Chrome auto-updates across all managed browsers — you set a minimum acceptable version and users cannot postpone or disable updates. Chrome releases a new stable version every 4 weeks; with CEP enforcement, your entire fleet can be on the latest version within hours of release. You can also configure a staged rollout — push to a test group first, validate, then fleet-wide. All enforced remotely from Admin Console.
additionalProtections: Update compliance reporting — real-time dashboard showing which devices are on which Chrome version
Block older insecure versions from accessing corporate Workspace apps via access policy
Zero user action required — silent background update, no pop-ups, no deferred restarts
deepDive.proofPoint: The average enterprise browser fleet lags 30–60 days behind the latest Chrome release. In that window, known CVEs with public exploits are actively used in attacks. CEP closes that gap.

answerChips.label: Mixed / uncontrolled browsers
answerChips.pitchBullet: CEP deploys a managed Chrome profile that coexists with personal browsers — no user change required, full IT policy control in the work profile
answerChips.sayThis: You can't enforce consistent security policies across Chrome, Edge, and Firefox simultaneously. CEP makes Chrome the managed work browser without asking anyone to switch their personal setup.

deepDive.howItWorks: CEP deploys as a managed Chrome profile alongside whatever browsers the user already has. The user's personal Chrome profile stays completely unchanged — their bookmarks, extensions, and history are untouched. The work Chrome profile (identifiable by a managed badge) enforces all IT policies: DLP, extension controls, safe browsing, site restrictions. For Microsoft shops, managed Chrome coexists with Edge — users can switch based on task. CEP doesn't remove anything; it adds a controlled work context.
additionalProtections: SSO integration — Entra ID or Workspace credentials auto-sign the work profile, no separate login
Works on Windows, Mac, Linux, and ChromeOS — the same managed profile experience on every device the user signs into
Profile switching is instant — users move between work and personal Chrome with one click
deepDive.proofPoint: The 'users won't change' objection dissolves here — they don't change anything. They get a second profile badge in their existing Chrome browser. Most users forget it's there within a week.

questions.question: Do any of your teams rely on Windows-based or legacy applications that need a specific environment to run?
questions.purpose: Surface the Cameyo opportunity. Any Windows or legacy app dependency — Citrix, RDS, local installs — is a direct use case for app delivery in the browser.
followUps: How are those apps delivered today — Citrix, RDS, local install?
How many users need access, and on what types of devices?
What's the current cost per seat for that infrastructure — licenses, servers, IT support?

industryTips.manufacturing: Manufacturing teams almost always have legacy ERP, MES, or SCADA systems that need Windows. Ask specifically about shop floor access.
industryTips.retail: Back-office and POS management tools are often Windows-only — Cameyo delivers these to any device, including Chromebooks in stores.
industryTips.professional-services: Ask about legacy time-billing, case management, or document management systems — these are often the last reason a firm maintains Windows infrastructure.

answerChips.label: Has Citrix / RDS / VDI
answerChips.pitchBullet: Cameyo replaces Citrix and RDS infrastructure entirely — Windows apps stream in a Chrome tab at 40–60% lower TCO, with zero servers to maintain
answerChips.sayThis: What are you paying per seat for Citrix today — including licenses, servers, and IT support? That number is usually where the conversation gets very interesting.

deepDive.howItWorks: Cameyo packages Windows applications as Progressive Web Apps (PWAs) — the app runs in a cloud-hosted Windows container; the user interacts with it via a Chrome tab. There's no RDP client, no plugin, no VPN tunnel. The application behaves identically to local — keyboard shortcuts, printing, file dialogs — because it IS running on Windows, just hosted in Cameyo's cloud infrastructure. For IT, management is a web console: upload the application, assign it to users or groups, done. Cameyo handles the Windows licensing, server patching, and uptime.
additionalProtections: All compute happens server-side — no corporate application data ever touches the endpoint device
Full session audit logging — every interaction with the application is logged for security and compliance
Works on Chromebook, iPad, thin client, personal laptop — any device with a Chrome browser, no Windows required on the endpoint
deepDive.proofPoint: Average Cameyo deployment: 3–5 days from decision to users running the app. Citrix migrations typically run 3–9 months and require dedicated infrastructure teams. TCO reduction vs Citrix: 40–60% in year one, including licence and infrastructure savings.

answerChips.label: Local Windows app installs
answerChips.pitchBullet: Cameyo delivers any Windows app as a cloud-hosted web session — nothing installed on end-user devices, works on any device including Chromebooks
answerChips.sayThis: Every local Windows install is an IT management overhead and a potential attack surface. Cameyo moves those apps to the cloud without rewriting a single line of code.

deepDive.howItWorks: Cameyo's packaging process captures an existing Windows application installer and virtualises it into a hosted cloud session. You run the app's installer through Cameyo's packaging wizard — it captures all dependencies, registry entries, and runtime requirements — and publishes it as a hosted app. No code changes, no redevelopment. IT manages one cloud-hosted instance; one update there updates it for all users simultaneously. Users access via a Chrome tab — the app appears fully functional within seconds.
additionalProtections: Eliminates endpoint-as-attack-surface risk — the application and its data never touch the user's local drive
Centralised licence management — one hosted instance, one licence to manage, no per-device installs to track or audit
Instant deprovisioning — remove a user's access to the application in one click from the Cameyo console
deepDive.proofPoint: Organisations migrating to Cameyo typically report 60–80% fewer application-related IT support tickets in the first quarter — no local installs means no installation failures, version conflicts, or device-specific compatibility issues.

answerChips.label: Legacy ERP / specialist software
answerChips.pitchBullet: Cameyo virtualises any application — SAP, legacy ERP, line-of-business tools — no rewrite or migration needed, users see no difference
answerChips.sayThis: The beauty of Cameyo is you never touch the application itself. It runs exactly as-is, streamed via Chrome. No redevelopment, no user retraining.

deepDive.howItWorks: Cameyo runs the application in a Windows Server environment in Cameyo's cloud. To the ERP or legacy software, it looks like a normal Windows desktop session — same OS version, same dependencies, same printer drivers. Users open a Chrome tab and the application appears, fully functional, within a few seconds. Cameyo handles all Windows Server licensing, patching, and infrastructure management. Your organisation never touches a server; the application just works.
additionalProtections: Isolation — legacy applications run in sandboxed cloud sessions, not on endpoints where they create security risk or compatibility conflicts
No endpoint OS dependency — legacy 32-bit apps run perfectly on modern hardware without compatibility shims
DLP at the session level — restrict copy-paste, clipboard, or file transfer within specific legacy application sessions for sensitive data controls
deepDive.proofPoint: Customers running SAP, AS/400, and other legacy ERP via Cameyo report zero end-user complaints about application behaviour — it works identically to a local install. One manufacturing customer moved 400 ERP users from Citrix to Cameyo in 8 days.

answerChips.label: Needs multi-device / thin client access
answerChips.pitchBullet: Cameyo runs those apps on any device — Chromebook, tablet, thin client — without modifying the application at all
answerChips.sayThis: Imagine giving shop floor workers or remote teams access to that application on a Chromebook that costs a fraction of a Windows laptop.

deepDive.howItWorks: Because the application runs in a cloud session and is accessed purely via Chrome, the endpoint device is completely irrelevant. A Chromebook, a personal Windows laptop, a MacBook, an iPad, or a thin client all deliver the same application experience — it streams from Cameyo's cloud containers regardless of local hardware. For organisations deploying Chromebooks to reduce hardware costs, Cameyo removes the last remaining blocker: 'but we still need Windows devices for [specific app]'.
additionalProtections: Session persistence — if a user loses their connection mid-session, they reconnect and the application session resumes exactly where they left off
Concurrent sessions — the same user on multiple devices is handled cleanly without licence conflicts
Offline PWA caching for some application types — light offline capability for users with intermittent connections
deepDive.proofPoint: Chromebooks cost 40–60% less than equivalent Windows hardware. For organisations with large frontline, seasonal, or high-turnover workforces, Cameyo + Chromebook is consistently the fastest TCO improvement in the first 12 months.

questions.question: What's the mix of company-managed devices versus personal, unmanaged, or contractor devices in your organisation?
questions.purpose: BYOD and unmanaged endpoints are the biggest gap CEP fills. Users on personal devices are completely outside IT's control today — and most customers haven't fully reckoned with this.
followUps: How do contractors or temps access your systems?
Can IT push security controls to a personal device?
When a contractor finishes, can you revoke access to data they've already downloaded?

industryTips.retail: High contractor and seasonal staff turnover means unmanaged device access and offboarding are real risks — and often completely unaddressed.
industryTips.professional-services: Contractors accessing client data from personal devices is a liability many firms haven't fully addressed — it's a DLP conversation waiting to happen.

answerChips.label: High BYOD / personal device use
answerChips.pitchBullet: CEP's managed Chrome profile enforces full corporate DLP and security policies inside the browser on personal devices — without MDM, without touching the personal side of the device
answerChips.sayThis: Right now, someone on a personal laptop could download your entire customer list to their desktop and IT would never know. CEP stops that without requiring MDM enrollment.

deepDive.howItWorks: CEP deploys a managed Chrome profile through your directory — when a user signs into managed Chrome with their work account on a personal device, the work profile appears. Inside that profile: all DLP controls, safe browsing enforcement, extension restrictions, and site policies apply exactly as they would on a corporate-managed device. Outside that profile — in the user's personal Chrome — nothing changes and nothing is monitored. IT cannot see personal browsing; the user cannot transfer corporate data outside the managed profile.
additionalProtections: No MDM enrollment required — the personal device never enters your MDM programme, avoiding privacy and consent complexities
Instant revocation — remove the user from your directory and the work profile on their personal device becomes inaccessible immediately
Screen capture DLP — prevent screenshots and screen recordings within the managed profile, even on personal devices
Download blocking — stop corporate documents being saved to personal device local storage
deepDive.proofPoint: Managed profile deployment takes under 5 minutes per user and requires no IT visit or physical access. The user just signs into Chrome with their work account on any device they own.

answerChips.label: Contractors / third-party access
answerChips.pitchBullet: Managed profiles can be provisioned for contractors in minutes and revoked the instant an engagement ends — no device enrollment, no data left behind
answerChips.sayThis: When a contractor finishes, can you revoke access to data they've already downloaded to their personal machine? That's the question most organisations can't answer.

deepDive.howItWorks: Contractor onboarding becomes a directory account creation — Google Workspace or Azure AD. Once created, the work profile appears on any device the contractor uses when they sign in. Your DLP policies, approved app access list, and security controls apply from day one. When the engagement ends, you deactivate the account — the work profile on their device becomes inaccessible immediately and all cached session data is gone. No physical device collection, no forensic verification, no manual data wipe needed.
additionalProtections: Data never touches the personal device — downloads blocked or silently redirected to corporate cloud storage rather than local disk
Session-level access only — contractors access approved web apps without ever touching the corporate network layer
Full audit trail — every site visited, file accessed, and download attempted during the engagement, available for review if a dispute arises
deepDive.proofPoint: For a typical 50-contractor engagement, onboarding and offboarding IT overhead drops from 2–3 days of device logistics to under 30 minutes of account management.

answerChips.label: No MDM on unmanaged devices
answerChips.pitchBullet: CEP applies corporate IT controls via a single managed Chrome profile — no MDM agent, no device enrollment, no changes to the device at all
answerChips.sayThis: You get full security controls without the MDM overhead. The user just opens a Chrome profile — IT has complete policy control inside it.

deepDive.howItWorks: MDM manages the device at the OS level — CEP manages the browser at the application level. These are completely separate security layers and CEP requires no MDM. The managed Chrome profile is a user-level construct: the user signs into Chrome with their work account, and policies enforce inside that browser context. No device enrollment, no MDM profile, no user consent for device-level monitoring. The managed profile is sandboxed from the rest of the device — IT controls what happens inside Chrome, nothing else.
additionalProtections: Compatible with locked-down devices where MDM enrollment is impossible — customer-owned devices, partner-managed machines, kiosk hardware
No device inventory or management overhead — only the browser profile is managed, not the underlying OS
If you add MDM later, CEP and MDM policies coexist — CEP handles browser-layer; MDM handles device-layer — without conflict
deepDive.proofPoint: CEP is the only enterprise security control that works identically on a fully managed corporate laptop, a contractor's personal MacBook, and a Chromebook on a public WiFi network — because security enforces at identity, not at device enrollment.

answerChips.label: Seasonal / temporary staff
answerChips.pitchBullet: Managed profiles for temporary staff are provisioned and deprovisioned in minutes — full controls during the engagement, nothing left behind on the device
answerChips.sayThis: Seasonal workers are one of the highest-risk groups for data leakage and credential misuse. Managed profiles fix that with zero infrastructure overhead per user.

deepDive.howItWorks: For each seasonal or temporary worker, you create a time-limited directory account and assign it to a policy group configured for that role. They sign into Chrome with that account on any available device — shared Chromebooks, their own laptop, or a kiosk device — and your policies apply immediately. At season end, deactivate the accounts (or configure automatic expiry). No laptop imaging, no device collection, no account cleanup. For shared devices, each user's session in their work profile is fully isolated from other users' sessions.
additionalProtections: Shared device mode — multiple temporary workers can use the same Chromebook with fully isolated, private sessions
Kiosk mode — lock a device to a single application (a warehouse fulfilment tool, a retail POS system) for roles that don't need general browser access
Automatic account expiry — set an end date on the account so offboarding happens without any manual IT action at season end
deepDive.proofPoint: Retailers using CEP for seasonal workforces report 80%+ reduction in IT overhead per seasonal hire compared to traditional Windows device provisioning and decommissioning cycles.

questions.question: Has your organisation experienced any security incidents, phishing attempts, or malware in the past 12 months — even near-misses?
questions.purpose: Establish security as a current, lived business problem rather than a theoretical risk. This is where urgency lives.
followUps: What was the impact — downtime, data loss, regulatory exposure?
Was the browser involved in any of those incidents?
Did you have visibility into what the user was doing in the browser at the time?

industryTips.financial-services: Financial services is the #1 target for phishing globally. Even a near-miss carries significant regulatory and reputational cost.
industryTips.technology: SaaS companies are increasingly targeted via browser-based credential harvesting and OAuth token theft — ask about those specifically.

answerChips.label: Had phishing / malware incidents
answerChips.pitchBullet: CEP's real-time threat protection blocks malicious sites and phishing pages at the browser layer — before credentials or data are submitted — not after the fact
answerChips.sayThis: The attacker doesn't need to breach your firewall if they can get your user to click a link in Chrome. CEP intercepts that before the user ever reaches the fake login page.

deepDive.howItWorks: CEP's real-time threat protection runs Safe Browsing checks against every URL before the page loads — comparing against Google's continuously updated threat intelligence database of malicious sites, phishing pages, and malware distribution points. For enterprise customers, Enhanced Protection mode sends additional real-time signals for faster detection of novel threats. This happens before any page content is rendered, before any credentials are entered, and before any file is served. It doesn't rely on the user identifying a suspicious URL.
additionalProtections: Malware scanning on downloads — files scanned against threat intelligence before they reach the endpoint filesystem
Password protection alerts — warns users if they enter a work credential on a non-corporate site, catching credential harvesting in real time
Zero-day phishing protection — even brand-new phishing domains with no prior history are risk-scored by ML models and blocked or flagged
deepDive.proofPoint: Google's Safe Browsing protects over 4 billion devices and blocks more than 3 million phishing pages per week. CEP's enterprise tier adds telemetry logging, policy control, and enhanced signals beyond what the consumer version provides.

answerChips.label: Browser not in incident response
answerChips.pitchBullet: CEP logs full browser-layer activity — sites visited, files downloaded, extensions active — giving your security team the forensic evidence needed to investigate fast
answerChips.sayThis: One thing we find consistently: when a breach happens, the browser timeline is the hardest thing to reconstruct. CEP changes that — every session is logged and searchable.

deepDive.howItWorks: CEP generates structured event logs for every managed browser session — sites visited, extensions installed and removed, file downloads (attempted, blocked, or completed), Safe Browsing events triggered, and password alerts. These logs are retained in Chrome Browser Cloud Management and exportable on demand. For SIEM integration, CEP forwards events via API connector to Chronicle, Splunk, CrowdStrike Falcon, or any SIEM with a webhook receiver — giving your SOC browser events in the same dashboard as endpoint and network telemetry.
additionalProtections: Timeline reconstruction — for any user, pull a complete browser session history for any date range in seconds
Alert policies — configure automatic SOC alerts for high-risk event patterns (e.g., user attempted 50+ file downloads in one hour)
Tamper-resistant logging — events are written to Google's infrastructure and cannot be modified by local administrators or compromised devices
deepDive.proofPoint: For organisations that have experienced a browser-based breach, the absence of browser logs is consistently the #1 factor cited by incident response teams that extended their investigation timeline — often from days to weeks.

answerChips.label: No visibility into browser activity
answerChips.pitchBullet: CEP feeds browser telemetry directly into Chronicle, Splunk, or your existing SIEM — so browser-layer events appear in your security monitoring alongside network and endpoint events
answerChips.sayThis: You can see network traffic, you can see endpoint events — but between the endpoint and the web, you're currently flying blind. That gap is exactly where modern attacks live.

deepDive.howItWorks: Chrome Browser Cloud Management provides a fleet-wide telemetry dashboard showing browser versions, installed extensions with risk scores, active policy compliance, Safe Browsing events, and DLP policy triggers — all in real time. The SIEM connector streams structured JSON events to your existing tools on a configurable event type filter — you forward only the signals your SOC cares about to avoid noise. Custom alert rules can be configured directly in the console: for example, 'alert if any user installs an extension with screen-recording permissions' or 'alert if download volume exceeds 500MB in a session.'
additionalProtections: Zero additional endpoint agents — all telemetry is native to Chrome, no instrumentation software to deploy or maintain
Policy compliance dashboard — instantly shows which devices and users are non-compliant with which specific policies
User risk profiling — surface users who are consistently triggering Safe Browsing or DLP events across sessions
deepDive.proofPoint: Security teams that instrument the browser layer with CEP telemetry typically discover 3–5x more security events than they were previously capturing — not because threats increased, but because the visibility gap is filled.

answerChips.label: Ransomware / malware download risk
answerChips.pitchBullet: CEP's download controls and malware scanning prevent malicious files reaching the endpoint — configurable by file type, domain, or user group
answerChips.sayThis: Ransomware almost always enters via a browser download or a phishing click. CEP blocks both at the source — before anything reaches the endpoint.

deepDive.howItWorks: Ransomware enters enterprise environments via two primary browser vectors: drive-by downloads (malicious files served from compromised or typosquatted sites) and phishing (credential theft leading to remote access or malicious document delivery). CEP intercepts both: Safe Browsing blocks the malicious URL before the file download is initiated; for phishing, the page is blocked before any credentials can be submitted. CEP's download controls add a second layer: you can restrict which file types can be downloaded from which site categories — blocking .exe, .zip, or macro-enabled Office files from uncategorised domains entirely.
additionalProtections: Site isolation — Chrome's sandboxed rendering limits what any single page can access on the device, containing potential exploits
Block file downloads from non-allowlisted domains — only approved sources can deliver executable or archive content to managed users
Rapid response — if a user's profile is suspected compromised, IT can remotely sign them out of managed Chrome across all devices instantly
deepDive.proofPoint: In Verizon's 2024 Data Breach Investigations Report, 68% of breaches involved a human element — primarily phishing clicks or stolen credentials. Both attack types are interceptable at the browser layer before they reach the endpoint or network.

answerChips.label: Travelling / remote users, no VPN
answerChips.pitchBullet: CEP enforces security at the browser layer regardless of network — travelling users get the same DLP, phishing protection, and policy enforcement on public WiFi or foreign networks as they would in the office
answerChips.sayThis: VPNs secure the tunnel — they don't control what happens inside the browser once you're through it. CEP's controls live inside Chrome itself, so whether your user is on hotel WiFi in Singapore or a coffee shop in London, the exact same policies apply. No VPN tunnel required for browser-layer protection.

deepDive.howItWorks: CEP's security model is identity-based, not network-based. When a user signs into managed Chrome with their work account, all policies enforce via the browser profile — regardless of what network they're on. Safe browsing runs against every URL, DLP rules apply to every download or upload, extension controls are active, and all events are logged — without traffic routing through a corporate proxy. For advanced zero-trust architectures, CEP integrates with BeyondCorp Enterprise to provide context-aware access: granting or restricting access to corporate apps based on browser management status, user identity, and device posture. No VPN required.
additionalProtections: HTTPS-only enforcement — CEP blocks protocol downgrades that attackers use for man-in-the-middle attacks on public WiFi
DNS-over-HTTPS — prevents DNS hijacking on compromised public or hotel networks
Certificate transparency — blocks access to sites with invalid or spoofed certificates
BeyondCorp context-aware access — replaces VPN with identity + managed-browser verification for Workspace apps
deepDive.proofPoint: Organisations moving from VPN-based access to CEP + BeyondCorp for remote workers typically reduce VPN helpdesk tickets by 70%+ while improving security posture — access is granted based on identity and browser management status, not network location.

questions.question: What controls do you have today to prevent sensitive data leaving via the browser — downloads to personal devices, uploads to personal cloud storage, copy-paste to external tools?
questions.purpose: DLP at the browser layer is a key CEP differentiator. Most organisations realise they have zero controls here once asked directly.
followUps: What happens if an employee copies a client list to their personal Google Drive or Dropbox?
Can IT audit what's been downloaded from your web applications?
How do you handle contractors who need to access sensitive data?

industryTips.financial-services: Browser-based DLP maps directly to PCI-DSS 3.x and FCA Consumer Duty expectations around data handling — it's a compliance investment, not just security.
industryTips.technology: Source code and IP exfiltration via personal cloud storage is a serious risk for SaaS companies — CEP's DLP controls can block this at the browser layer.

answerChips.label: No download controls
answerChips.pitchBullet: CEP enforces download policy by file type, destination, or user group — blocking, logging, or watermarking files as they leave corporate web applications
answerChips.sayThis: Right now, anyone could download your entire customer database via the browser to an unmanaged device and IT would never know. CEP stops that and logs every attempt.

deepDive.howItWorks: CEP's DLP download rules are configured in Chrome Admin Console and enforce at the browser profile level. Rules can trigger based on: file type (block .xlsx, .pdf, .csv), source domain (block downloads from non-corporate cloud storage), destination (block to local storage on unmanaged devices), or content inspection (CEP's DLP scanner detects credit card patterns, NI numbers, or custom regex in files being downloaded). When a rule fires, the download is blocked or redirected, a configurable policy message is shown to the user, and the event is logged with full context.
additionalProtections: Watermarking mode — instead of blocking, silently add a visible watermark to sensitive documents on download so exfiltrated files are traceable back to the source user
Print DLP — apply the same controls to printing sensitive web app content
Clipboard DLP — simultaneously block copy-paste of content from the same sensitive pages
deepDive.proofPoint: Most organisations find, after deploying CEP DLP rules, that 5–10 downloads per user per week would have been flagged by a standard policy — many of which the user making the download wouldn't have considered a policy issue.

answerChips.label: No personal cloud upload controls
answerChips.pitchBullet: CEP blocks or alerts on uploads to personal cloud storage — personal Google Drive, Dropbox, OneDrive — while allowing approved corporate destinations through
answerChips.sayThis: If someone copies a confidential document to their personal Dropbox right now, would IT know? CEP blocks that and records the attempt — silently, with no user friction.

deepDive.howItWorks: CEP monitors all file upload events at the browser layer and can block uploads to specific domains — personal Google Drive (drive.google.com) while allowing the corporate Workspace domain, Dropbox, Box, WeTransfer, or any domain not on an approved allowlist. The control is in the browser, not at the network layer — it applies on home networks, mobile hotspots, and public WiFi where a corporate proxy would not intercept the traffic. The user sees a configurable policy message; the attempt is logged for the security team.
additionalProtections: Real-time content scanning — detect whether a file being uploaded contains sensitive data patterns before the upload is blocked or allowed
Approved channels enforcement — users are automatically guided to use corporate Workspace, SharePoint, or Teams instead of blocked personal destinations
DLP incident dashboard — all blocked upload attempts visible to the security team in real time, with user, file type, and destination recorded
deepDive.proofPoint: For organisations with significant contractor populations, upload controls are often the single highest-impact CEP DLP control — they close the personal cloud exfiltration channel that traditional network-layer DLP tools miss when users are on home or mobile networks.

answerChips.label: No copy-paste / clipboard controls
answerChips.pitchBullet: CEP's clipboard policies prevent sensitive data being copied from corporate web apps and pasted into personal apps, emails, or unapproved external tools
answerChips.sayThis: Copy-paste is the most underestimated data exfiltration channel. A user can walk out with an entire salary spreadsheet in their clipboard, and nothing flags it today.

deepDive.howItWorks: CEP's clipboard DLP policy can prevent copy operations from within specified web applications — block copy-paste from your HR system, CRM, banking portal, or any designated sensitive web app to applications outside the managed Chrome profile. You can allow copy within the managed profile (internal app to internal app) while blocking paste to personal email drafts, external tools, Slack, or AI assistants. Controls are configurable by source application URL pattern, destination type, or data content classification.
additionalProtections: Screenshot prevention — block Print Screen and screenshotting tools within the managed profile for applications designated as sensitive
Print restriction — prevent printing from specific web applications that handle confidential data
Session recording for privileged roles — optional full session recording for administrator or finance accounts accessing sensitive applications, with audit trail
deepDive.proofPoint: Clipboard-based data exfiltration generates zero network traffic — it is completely invisible to DLP systems that inspect packets or proxy connections. It is only catchable at the browser layer, which is exactly where CEP enforces.

answerChips.label: No data movement audit trail
answerChips.pitchBullet: CEP generates a full audit log of browser-layer data events — downloads, uploads, clipboard use — ready for compliance reporting and forensic investigation
answerChips.sayThis: When your auditor asks 'how do you know sensitive data isn't leaving via the browser?' — this is the answer. CEP gives you the evidence trail that's currently missing.

deepDive.howItWorks: CEP generates structured audit events for every DLP-relevant browser action: file downloads (allowed and blocked), file uploads, clipboard operations in restricted apps, print events, and Safe Browsing triggers. Each event includes user identity, device identifier, timestamp, source URL, file name and type, and the specific policy that triggered. Events are retained in Chrome Browser Cloud Management and can be exported via API or forwarded to a SIEM. For compliance reporting, queries like 'show all download events for user X in the last 30 days' execute in seconds.
additionalProtections: Immutable log storage — events are written to Google's infrastructure and cannot be modified by local IT administrators or compromised accounts
Pre-built compliance report templates for PCI-DSS, ISO 27001, FCA, and SOC 2 audit requirements
Automated alert escalation — configure notifications to the security team or compliance officer for high-severity DLP events in real time
deepDive.proofPoint: A financial services customer using CEP produced all browser-layer evidence required for a PCI audit in 4 hours. Without CEP, their prior audit required 3 weeks of manual log reconstruction from proxy and endpoint data that still had significant gaps.

questions.question: Does your IT team have any visibility into what's happening inside the browser today — sites visited, extensions installed, file downloads, data transfers?
questions.purpose: Surface the visibility gap. IT can often see the network perimeter but is completely blind inside the browser — this is the insight that creates urgency.
followUps: If a user installs a malicious extension today, would IT know?
Is browser activity included in your SIEM or security monitoring?
How long would it take to detect a data exfiltration via the browser?

answerChips.label: No browser event logging
answerChips.pitchBullet: CEP reports browser telemetry to Chronicle, Splunk, or any SIEM — extension events, download attempts, safe browsing blocks — all visible in existing security dashboards
answerChips.sayThis: You can see your firewalls, your endpoints, your cloud logs — but inside the browser, you're currently blind. CEP fills that last mile of visibility.

deepDive.howItWorks: Chrome Browser Cloud Management collects and forwards structured telemetry events via a connector to your SIEM. Event types include: URL visited (with Google-classified category), extension installs and removals, download events (allowed and blocked), Safe Browsing trigger events, DLP policy fires, and password alerts. The connector supports Chronicle (native Google integration), Splunk (via HTTP Event Collector), and any SIEM with an API or webhook receiver. Initial connector setup takes 1–2 hours; once live, browser events appear in your existing dashboards alongside endpoint and network events.
additionalProtections: Custom event filters — forward only high-priority events to your SIEM to avoid alert fatigue and reduce ingest costs
User behaviour analytics ready — browser telemetry feeds UEBA models with session data to identify anomalous user activity patterns
Raw log export for retention — export full event logs to your own cloud storage for long-term compliance archiving
deepDive.proofPoint: SOC teams that add CEP browser telemetry to their SIEM typically see mean-time-to-detect on browser-based threats drop from weeks to hours — the gap in telemetry is filled, and previously invisible events start generating alerts.

answerChips.label: No extension inventory / audit
answerChips.pitchBullet: CEP gives IT a real-time inventory of every extension installed across the entire fleet — risk-scored, flagged, and controllable centrally from one dashboard
answerChips.sayThis: If I asked you right now what extensions your 200 users have installed, could you answer? One of those could be logging every keystroke on every web app they open.

deepDive.howItWorks: The Chrome Admin Console provides a continuously updated inventory of every extension installed across your managed fleet. For each extension you see: name, version, publisher, Chrome Web Store link, permissions requested (plain-language, e.g., 'read and change all data on all websites'), install date, and user and device counts. You can block a specific extension by ID with a single click — the extension is removed from all affected devices within 15 minutes. Allowlist mode prevents any new extension installs without explicit IT approval — users are shown a request flow instead.
additionalProtections: New extension install alerts — get an immediate notification when any managed user installs an extension not on your approved list
Extension permission change monitoring — flag when a trusted extension receives an update that expands its permission scope
Shadow IT discovery — extensions reveal unsanctioned tools in use: personal AI writing assistants reading document content, personal cloud storage sync tools, productivity trackers
deepDive.proofPoint: In a typical 500-seat first extension audit, organisations discover an average of 12–15 high-risk extensions installed across the fleet — including screen recorders, in-browser credential vaults, and personal productivity extensions with permissions to read all page content.

answerChips.label: Slow to detect anomalies
answerChips.pitchBullet: CEP's analytics flag anomalous patterns — unusual download spikes, new extension installs, access to blocked categories — in real time, not days later in a log review
answerChips.sayThis: The average time to detect a browser-based breach is 197 days. CEP compresses that dramatically because it's watching in real time, not flagging after the fact.

deepDive.howItWorks: CEP's reporting dashboard includes pre-built anomaly detection views for the most common high-risk patterns: large download volume spikes, access to blocked site categories, DLP policy trigger clusters, and new extension installs across multiple users simultaneously. Custom alert rules are configured in the console using event type and threshold criteria — for example, 'fire alert if any user attempts to download more than 50 files within one hour' or 'alert if download volume from any single user exceeds 500MB in a session.' Alerts fire in real time and can route to email, webhook, or your SIEM.
additionalProtections: UEBA integration — browser activity patterns feed directly into User and Entity Behaviour Analytics tools (CrowdStrike Falcon, Splunk UBA, Microsoft Sentinel) if deployed
Peer group baseline comparison — flag users who deviate significantly from their cohort's browser behaviour patterns
Time-based access policies — restrict access to sensitive site categories or applications outside defined business hours
deepDive.proofPoint: The median time to detect insider threats via traditional log review is 197 days. With real-time browser-layer alerting configured in CEP, anomalous data exfiltration patterns are typically flagged within 24 hours.

answerChips.label: Compliance visibility gap
answerChips.pitchBullet: CEP audit logs satisfy browser-layer visibility requirements in PCI-DSS, FCA, SOC2, and ISO27001 — with exportable reports ready for auditors
answerChips.sayThis: When the auditor asks 'show me your browser controls' — most organisations have nothing to show. CEP makes that question very easy to answer.

deepDive.howItWorks: CEP provides exportable audit evidence covering the browser-layer control requirements most commonly cited in enterprise compliance frameworks. PCI-DSS: cardholder data access controls, download restrictions, and audit logs for web application access. FCA and UK financial regulation: data handling evidence, DLP controls, and user activity logs. SOC 2: logical access controls, DLP configuration evidence, and security event logs. ISO 27001: browser security policy enforcement documentation and continuous monitoring evidence. All reports are generated from Chrome Browser Cloud Management and can be exported in CSV or accessed via API for automated evidence collection.
additionalProtections: Policy attestation reports — document which security policies are active, enforced, and applied to which user groups at any point in time
Chrome Security Assessment — Google's tooling shows your current browser security posture against CIS benchmark baselines with remediation recommendations
Auditor-ready dashboards — pre-built report views designed for evidence submission format, not just internal monitoring use
deepDive.proofPoint: A financial services firm using CEP reduced their browser security audit preparation time from three weeks of manual log reconstruction to four hours of report export — the evidence is pre-structured, timestamped, and available on demand.

stages.name: Pitch
stages.description: Present Chrome Enterprise Premium and Cameyo as the browser-first platform that solves exactly what you uncovered in discovery. Lead with their pain, not product features.

talkingPoints.title: Chrome Enterprise Premium: the browser is your new security perimeter
talkingPoints.content: Your team already spends 80%+ of their working day in a browser. Chrome Enterprise Premium turns that surface into a zero-trust security perimeter — policy-controlled, threat-protected, and DLP-enforced. No VPN required. No new hardware. Deploys to existing Chrome browsers in hours.
tips: Lead with: 'The browser is now the #1 attack surface — yet most security budgets allocate less than 10% to it'
Ask: 'What does your current security stack cost per user vs. what CEP adds — and what's the cost of one incident?'
For Google Workspace customers: mention they may already have entitlements included

industryTips.financial-services: CEP's DLP and screen-capture controls map directly to PCI-DSS and FCA requirements — frame it as a compliance investment. Regulators are increasingly asking about browser controls.
industryTips.technology: For SaaS companies, CEP protects against OAuth token theft and credential harvesting — the #1 attack vector against tech-forward teams.

talkingPoints.title: Cameyo: your Windows apps in a Chrome tab — no Citrix, no VDI infrastructure
talkingPoints.content: Any application that runs on Windows can be delivered as a web app via Cameyo. Users open a Chrome tab, the app streams from the cloud. No servers to build or maintain. No client software to install. Typical deployment: days, not months. Customers replace Citrix at 40–60% lower TCO.
tips: If they have Citrix: get the per-seat cost before presenting Cameyo pricing — the TCO comparison is the close
Key message: 'Your users don't change anything — the Windows app just opens in a tab'
Speed angle: 'You could have 10 users running [specific app name] by end of this week'

industryTips.manufacturing: Position as a Citrix replacement for ERP and MES access — especially for shop floor workers who need one app, not a full Windows desktop.
industryTips.retail: Ideal for delivering back-office and POS management tools to Chromebooks or thin clients in stores — zero Windows infrastructure required.

talkingPoints.title: Corporate browser controls on personal and unmanaged devices — without touching the device
talkingPoints.content: For BYOD, contractor, and remote workers, CEP deploys a managed Chrome profile that enforces corporate policies within the browser, while leaving the personal side of the device completely untouched. Users get their personal Chrome profile; IT controls the work profile. The change is invisible to users; the control is real for IT.
tips: This is often the 'wow' moment for HR and legal — corporate DLP on personal devices without MDM is a novel concept for most customers
Ask first: 'What's your current policy for contractors accessing client data from their own laptop?' — silence is the answer you want

talkingPoints.title: Hours to deploy — not a 6-month infrastructure project
talkingPoints.content: CEP policy rollout to existing Chrome browsers takes hours. If the customer is on Google Workspace, many settings are pre-configured. Cameyo app onboarding is days. Compare that to a Citrix or traditional VDI rollout — which typically runs 3–9 months and requires dedicated infrastructure teams.
tips: Speed is a major differentiator vs. legacy security and virtualisation vendors
Good pilot close: 'What if we had 10 users running [specific use case] by end of next week?'
For sceptical IT buyers: low-risk pilot is more compelling than a long business case

talkingPoints.title: Works with what you already have — Google Workspace or Microsoft
talkingPoints.content: For Google Workspace customers, the Chrome Admin Console is included and may already have licences. For Microsoft-first environments, CEP integrates with Entra ID (Azure AD) via SAML/SCIM and works alongside Intune for device context. No new identity platform. No rip-and-replace.
tips: For Workspace customers: 'You may already be paying for this — let's check your entitlements'
For Microsoft shops: don't fight the stack — show how CEP augments Entra and Intune rather than replacing them
Cameyo similarly integrates with Azure AD and Okta for SSO

industryTips.technology: SaaS-native companies on Google Workspace likely already own the Chrome Admin Console — CEP is an uplift to infrastructure they're already paying for.

stages.name: Objections
stages.description: Address concerns about incumbent solutions, cost, change management, and IT complexity. Stay curious — every objection is information.

objections.objection: We already manage endpoints with Intune / SCCM / MDM
objections.response: Intune manages the device — what happens inside the browser is invisible to it. CEP fills exactly that gap: browser-layer DLP, threat protection, and policy enforcement that Intune simply can't see. They're additive, not competing. Intune handles device compliance; CEP handles what happens at the application layer. You keep everything you have and gain visibility where you currently have none.
tips: Ask: 'Can Intune tell you what your users are downloading from the web today?'
The 'additive, not competing' framing is essential — don't position as a replacement
Follow up with the visibility gap question: 'If a user installs a malicious extension tomorrow, how long until IT knows?'

objections.objection: We're a Microsoft shop — we use Edge / M365 / Azure
objections.response: Completely valid — and CEP is built for exactly this. It integrates natively with Entra ID, Intune, and Azure AD, so you're not abandoning Microsoft; you're adding a browser-layer security control that augments your existing stack. Managed Chrome and managed Edge can also coexist on the same machine for different use cases. A 30-day pilot on 10 users costs nothing to find out whether the added security layer is worth it.
tips: Never fight the Microsoft stack — always position as complementary
If Edge commitment is immovable, pivot to Cameyo as the entry point instead
Key question: 'Does managed Edge give you DLP controls at the browser layer today?'

objections.objection: We tried VDI / Citrix before and it was too complex or expensive
objections.response: That pain is exactly why Cameyo was built. Traditional VDI means servers, sizing, licensing, and months of project work. Cameyo is fully SaaS — no infrastructure to build or maintain, no upfront capital spend. Customers go from decision to users streaming apps in under a week. The complexity and cost you experienced with Citrix is the specific problem Cameyo eliminates.
tips: Validate the pain first: 'What made it painful — cost, performance, ongoing maintenance?'
Then: 'What if you could deliver the same apps with no servers and no infrastructure team?'
Offer a proof: 'We could have a test environment running in 24 hours — want to see it?'

objections.objection: My users won't change browsers / we can't force a tool change on the business
objections.response: Users don't change anything. CEP deploys as a managed profile alongside their existing Chrome install — they keep their bookmarks, extensions, history, and muscle memory. For Cameyo, the Windows app opens in a Chrome tab. Most users don't know they're in a virtual session. The change is entirely invisible to end users; the security control is entirely visible to IT. That's the point.
tips: Lead with: 'This is an IT control, not a user-facing product change'
Good closer: 'What if we ran a 10-user pilot and asked them for feedback after a week?'
For orgs with a strong Microsoft culture: point out that managed Chrome profile doesn't replace Edge — it coexists

objections.objection: We're not ready to budget for this / it's not a priority this year
objections.response: Understood — let's make sure you have the numbers to build the case when the window opens. What does your current Citrix or VDI cost per user per year, including support and infrastructure? And what's the cost of one security incident for your organisation — downtime, remediation, regulatory fine? For most customers, CEP pays for itself within the first avoided incident. We can build that TCO model together so it's ready when budget comes around.
tips: This is a 'not yet' not a 'no' — keep the deal warm
Offer a business case / TCO template as a leave-behind
Identify whether the blocker is budget cycle timing or internal priority — different solutions
Ask: 'When does your budget cycle open — and what would you need to have ready for it?'

objections.objection: We already have security covered — antivirus, firewall, VPN
objections.response: Those are network perimeter controls — they protect the edge. But most modern attacks live entirely inside the browser: phishing via legitimate-looking sites, drive-by downloads, malicious extensions, OAuth token theft. None of these trip a firewall. Antivirus can't see what a user does in a web application. CEP fills the security gap that sits between the network perimeter and the user's fingers — and it's the fastest-growing attack surface in enterprise today.
tips: Stat to use: 'The browser is involved in over 80% of enterprise security incidents — yet most security stacks are blind inside it'
Ask: 'Does your current security stack give you visibility into browser-level activity — downloads, extension installs, site visits?'
Don't dismiss their stack — acknowledge it, then show what it can't see

stages.name: Close
stages.description: Confirm the use case, identify the buying committee, and land on a clear pilot or commercial next step. The goal is a specific commitment, not a vague 'let's keep talking'.

questions.question: Based on what we've discussed, does [browser security / legacy app delivery / both] resonate as the right starting point for your organisation?
questions.purpose: Get verbal confirmation on the primary use case before moving to commercial discussion. This surfaces any remaining misalignment early.
followUps: Is there a specific team, office, or user group that would make the most compelling pilot?
Is the priority security, cost reduction, or both?
Does IT or the business own this decision?

questions.question: What would success look like for you in the first 90 days of a deployment?
questions.purpose: Anchor the conversation to measurable outcomes and surface any unstated success criteria or stakeholder expectations.
followUps: Who internally would define and measure success?
Is there a security review, IT audit, or compliance milestone this could feed into?
What would make this an easy internal sell to your IT leadership?

questions.question: Who else needs to be part of the technical evaluation or commercial conversation — IT, security, procurement, a CISO?
questions.purpose: Multi-thread the deal and identify the full buying committee before a proposal goes in. Single-threaded deals die at procurement.
followUps: Is there a CISO or IT director who would need to sign off?
Does procurement get involved at this stage or later?
Is there an internal security review or vendor evaluation process?

questions.question: Would a 30-day, 10-seat pilot help build internal confidence and give your IT team a chance to evaluate the platform hands-on?
questions.purpose: Low-friction next step that creates forward momentum and surfaces any remaining blockers in a safe, reversible way.
followUps: Which use case would you want to validate — browser security, app delivery, or both?
Who on the IT team would own the technical evaluation?
What does the internal sign-off process look like to get a pilot approved?

talkingPoints.title: Proposed pilot structure
talkingPoints.content: 10 users, 30 days, one focused use case (browser security or app delivery), evaluated by the IT owner. No infrastructure investment. Clear success criteria agreed upfront. At day 30, review against criteria and decide together on commercial pathway.
tips: Keep it simple — one use case, one team, clear criteria
Offer to write the internal business case for them if needed
Pilot momentum beats perfect business case every time for SMB

talkingPoints.title: Commercial pathway from pilot to deployment
talkingPoints.content: Pilot → internal review → commercial agreement → phased rollout. For sub-1000 seat customers, full deployment is typically achievable within 60–90 days of commercial sign-off. No professional services engagement required for standard deployments.
tips: Emphasise speed-to-value for smaller customers — this isn't an 18-month enterprise project
If procurement is involved: get them a written proposal template early
Cameyo + CEP together: position as a bundled browser platform deal, not two separate purchases

closeRecommendations.rationale: They have specific legacy app dependencies — a hands-on POC is far more persuasive than a deck.
closeRecommendations.askThis: Based on what you've described with your legacy applications, the most valuable next step would be a quick technical POC. We can set up a sandbox environment where your team sees those apps running through Cameyo in a browser — no VDI, no client to install. Would it be worth 30 minutes of your architect's time this week?

closeRecommendations.rationale: Security and browser management gaps are most compellingly shown live — screenshots don't do it justice.
closeRecommendations.askThis: The best way to make this concrete is a live demo — I'll show you the Chrome Admin Console in action, walk through how policies deploy fleet-wide in under 15 minutes, and demonstrate the DLP controls so you can see exactly what visibility looks like. I can bring in our SE. Does Thursday work, or would early next week be better?

closeRecommendations.rationale: Unmanaged device problem is well-defined — they need commercials to take it forward internally.
closeRecommendations.askThis: It sounds like you've got a clear picture of the problem already — a lot of unmanaged devices and no clean way to enforce security on them today. The right move is probably a proposal so you can take something concrete to your IT team or budget holder. Let me put together a scoped option based on what you've told me — is it worth me getting that to you by Friday?

closeRecommendations.rationale: Compliance-driven prospects need a deeper conversation with the right technical resources before we can be specific.
closeRecommendations.askThis: There's more I'd want to understand about your compliance framework before I can be genuinely useful. Would it be worth getting our compliance specialist on a short call — say 30 minutes — to map out exactly where the browser layer fits into your audit requirements? I can set that up for next week.

talkingPoints.title: Regulatory compliance (FCA / PCI-DSS)
talkingPoints.content: Unmanaged browsers are a routine finding in FCA supervisory reviews and PCI-DSS audits. The browser is where card data, client data, and trading instructions flow — and most firms have no controls on it. CEP closes that gap with enforceable policies and a full audit trail.
tips: Ask about their last PCI or FCA audit — whether browser controls came up
Reference FCA Consumer Duty: firms must evidence controls over data handling end-to-end
PCI-DSS v4.0 explicitly requires controls on browser-based access to cardholder data

talkingPoints.title: Data leakage via personal cloud uploads
talkingPoints.content: In financial services, employees routinely handle price-sensitive information, client portfolios, and M&A data. Without DLP controls on the browser, a user can upload a spreadsheet to personal Google Drive or Dropbox in seconds — with no audit trail and no alert.
tips: Ask if they have controls preventing uploads to personal cloud storage from managed devices
Market abuse and insider trading risk applies here — not just data breach
CEP DLP rules can be set by file type, destination, or data classification

talkingPoints.title: Trading / dealing desk browser controls
talkingPoints.content: Dealing desks often run legacy applications alongside web-based trading platforms. Cameyo virtualises legacy apps so they run browser-side — no thick client, no VDI, no separate managed build for traders.
tips: Ask if any trading platforms still require Windows thick clients or Java
Reference the complexity of managing multiple desktop images for different desk types
Cameyo is FCA-audit-friendly: all app access is browser-based and logable

talkingPoints.title: Developer-targeted browser attacks
talkingPoints.content: Tech companies are high-value targets for browser-based credential theft. OAuth token hijacking and browser session theft are how attackers move laterally after the initial compromise. Developers are disproportionately targeted because their accounts have elevated access to source code and cloud infrastructure.
tips: Ask if they've had any OAuth token theft or session hijacking incidents
Reference the recent wave of developer-targeted infostealer malware
CEP Chrome Safe Browsing Enterprise catches these at the browser level before exfiltration

talkingPoints.title: SaaS sprawl and shadow IT
talkingPoints.content: Tech companies accumulate SaaS tools fast. CEP gives IT full visibility into which web applications employees are accessing — useful for licence management, shadow IT discovery, and data governance.
tips: Ask how they currently track which SaaS tools staff are using
CEP URL reporting shows every domain accessed across the managed fleet
Useful for SOC 2 Type II evidence as well as general hygiene

talkingPoints.title: Contractor and remote access without VPN
talkingPoints.content: Tech companies frequently use contractors, agencies, and remote staff who work on personal devices. Without VDI or managed devices, you have no control over their browser. CEP Managed Profile gives contractors a secure work context in Chrome — IT-controlled, without touching their personal device.
tips: Ask what percentage of their workforce is contractors or agency staff
Current solutions are usually VPN (fragile, expensive) or nothing
CEP profile approach is self-service: contractor installs Chrome, signs in with work email, policies apply instantly

talkingPoints.title: PCI-DSS at the point of sale
talkingPoints.content: Retail is squarely in scope for PCI-DSS. The browser is increasingly the POS interface — web-based checkouts, web POS systems, and order management all run browser-side. Unmanaged browsers in POS environments create PCI scope creep and audit risk.
tips: Ask whether their POS or order management system runs in a browser
PCI requires controls on what can be accessed from a device that touches cardholder data
CEP kiosk mode locks a browser to a specific URL — commonly used for POS terminals

talkingPoints.title: Seasonal and temporary workforce
talkingPoints.content: Retail hires thousands of seasonal staff who need quick, secure access to systems — and then need to be off-boarded just as fast. Managed Chrome profiles make this instant: the rep logs in with their work account, gets full IT-controlled access, and the profile is wiped when they leave.
tips: Ask how they currently onboard / offboard seasonal staff to IT systems
Current pain: IT tickets, device provisioning queues, forgotten offboarding
CEP + Google Workspace = self-service onboarding in minutes, not days

talkingPoints.title: Legacy ERP and production software
talkingPoints.content: Manufacturing runs on legacy — SAP, Oracle ERP, MES systems, bespoke production scheduling tools. Most were never designed for modern devices. Cameyo virtualises these so they run in a Chrome browser tab with no local install, no VDI infrastructure, and no dependency on Windows build versions.
tips: Ask what ERP or production scheduling software they run
Ask about the pain of managing Windows builds for shop floor vs office staff
Reference the cost of maintaining Citrix or RDS just to deliver one or two legacy apps

talkingPoints.title: Shop floor / thin client modernisation
talkingPoints.content: Chromebooks and thin clients running Chrome OS are increasingly replacing ageing Windows PCs on the factory floor. They're cheaper, more secure (no local software to patch), and centrally managed via the Chrome Admin Console. CEP turns this into a fully manageable, locked-down fleet.
tips: Ask about the age and management overhead of their shop floor terminals
Reference the Zero Trust angle: no local attack surface on a Chrome OS device
CEP kiosk mode can lock shop floor terminals to specific production apps only

talkingPoints.title: Client confidentiality and data controls
talkingPoints.content: Agencies, consultancies, and law firms handle highly sensitive client data — commercially sensitive strategies, legal privileged documents, financial models. The browser is the primary work surface, and without DLP controls, client data can leak via personal uploads, clipboard, or printing.
tips: Ask whether they have any controls preventing client files being uploaded to personal cloud storage
Legal privilege is lost if data is mishandled — frame as professional liability
CEP screen capture controls, download restrictions, and clipboard DLP all apply here

talkingPoints.title: BYOD for senior staff and partners
talkingPoints.content: Professional services firms often resist managing partner and senior employee devices. CEP Managed Profile solves this: IT manages the work browser profile, not the device. Partners keep their personal MacBook or Windows laptop; IT gets control over the work context only.
tips: Address the cultural resistance to MDM head-on: 'We're not managing the device, just the work profile'
Partners are often the highest-risk users — they have the most sensitive data access
Works on Mac, Windows, and Chrome OS without touching personal files or apps

openerStyles.label: Consultative
openerStyles.opener: Hi {{name}}, great to connect — thanks for making the time. Before I tell you anything about what we do, I'd love to understand your setup first. It means anything I share will actually be relevant to you rather than a generic pitch. Would that be OK?
openerStyles.agenda: My plan for today: a few questions about how your team handles browsers, applications, and security — then I'll share specifically what Chrome Enterprise Premium and Cameyo do — and we'll decide together whether there's a fit. Does that sound useful?
openerStyles.bridge: Great. Let me start with browsers — it's usually where we find the most interesting gaps. How are browsers managed across {{company}} today?

openerStyles.label: Challenger
openerStyles.opener: Hi {{name}}, thanks for making time. I looked at a few companies your size before this call and there are usually two or three things around browser security and application delivery that create real risk — often without anyone on the IT side realising it until it becomes a problem. I'd like to ask you a few questions to see whether any of those apply to {{company}}.
openerStyles.agenda: I'll keep it focused — a few questions about your environment, then a targeted view of where Chrome Enterprise Premium and Cameyo fit in. If it's not relevant, I'll tell you straight and we can wrap up early. Fair?
openerStyles.bridge: Let me start here: how are browsers managed across your organisation today — does IT push policies centrally, or are users largely running whatever they like?

openerStyles.label: Direct
openerStyles.opener: Hi {{name}}, good to speak. Quick intro — I work with companies your size on two things: securing the browser layer across managed and unmanaged devices, and modernising legacy Windows application delivery without VDI infrastructure. Rather than pitch you straight away, three quick questions to understand your setup, and I'll be honest about whether we can actually help.
openerStyles.agenda: Questions about your environment, then what Chrome Enterprise Premium and Cameyo do, then an honest conversation about fit. Shouldn't take long.
openerStyles.bridge: First question: how are browsers managed at {{company}} today?

openerStyles.label: Warm
openerStyles.opener: Hi {{name}}, really good to meet you. I always find these calls are much more useful when I take the time to understand what someone's actually dealing with day-to-day before I start talking about products. So if it's OK with you, I'd love to hear a bit about your setup first.
openerStyles.agenda: I'll ask a few questions about your environment, then share what Chrome Enterprise Premium and Cameyo can do, and we'll have a proper conversation about whether there's a fit. No pressure — just a useful chat. Sound good?
openerStyles.bridge: Brilliant. So tell me about {{company}} — how are things managed from a browser and application standpoint today? Centralised IT, or more distributed?

useCaseQuestions.question: How are your contractors or third-party workers currently accessing company resources?
useCaseQuestions.rationale: Unmanaged contractor access is the most common entry point for data loss. Identifying the gap surfaces urgency.
useCaseQuestions.followUp: What happens when a contractor's engagement ends — how do you revoke access?

useCaseQuestions.question: Do you have visibility into what contractors are doing in your systems once they're in?
useCaseQuestions.rationale: Lack of audit trail is a compliance and security risk. Pins the conversation to control, not just access.

useCaseQuestions.question: How many of your employees are using personal devices to access work apps or email?
useCaseQuestions.rationale: BYOD scope question — helps quantify the problem and identify whether this is an edge case or widespread.
useCaseQuestions.followUp: Are those devices enrolled in any MDM, or are they completely unmanaged?

useCaseQuestions.question: If an employee left tomorrow using their personal device, could you prevent them taking company data with them?
useCaseQuestions.rationale: Reframes BYOD risk from convenience to offboarding — a concrete, relatable scenario that drives urgency.

useCaseQuestions.question: What's your biggest concern around sensitive data leaving your organisation — through email, downloads, or the browser?
useCaseQuestions.rationale: Open-ended DLP question that surfaces whether the customer is thinking about egress at all.
useCaseQuestions.followUp: Is this something your compliance or legal team has flagged as a risk?

useCaseQuestions.question: Do you have a policy on which AI tools employees can use, and what data they're allowed to put into them?
useCaseQuestions.rationale: Most companies don't — this opens the Gen AI governance conversation quickly.
useCaseQuestions.followUp: What would be the consequence if an employee pasted customer data into a public AI tool?

useCaseQuestions.question: Have you had any incidents with phishing, malicious links, or employees hitting harmful sites?
useCaseQuestions.rationale: Incident history creates emotional context — past pain is the best predictor of purchase urgency.
useCaseQuestions.followUp: Was that on managed devices, personal devices, or both?

useCaseQuestions.question: Do your employees access the internet directly, or does traffic go through any filtering or proxy today?
useCaseQuestions.rationale: Establishes the baseline. If there's nothing, the gap is obvious. If there's something, understand whether it covers unmanaged devices.

useCaseQuestions.question: What's your current setup for delivering Windows applications to your users — Citrix, RDS, something else?
useCaseQuestions.rationale: Identifies the incumbent. Citrix/RDS means TCO and complexity conversation. Nothing means greenfield.
useCaseQuestions.followUp: When did you last evaluate whether that setup is still the right one?

useCaseQuestions.question: What are the main Windows apps you need to deliver — are they line-of-business apps, or more general productivity tools?
useCaseQuestions.rationale: Scopes the Cameyo use case. Helps identify whether this is a migration or a new delivery need.

useCaseQuestions.question: What's driving the need to look at this now — is there a project, a renewal, or a specific incident that's pushed this up the priority list?
useCaseQuestions.rationale: Universal timeline/urgency question. The answer shapes every other part of the call.

useCaseQuestions.question: What does success look like for you — if we solve this, what does your day-to-day look like six months from now?
useCaseQuestions.rationale: Future-state question that moves from problem to outcome. Generates language for the pitch and close.

pitchCards.title: Contractor Access Without the Risk
pitchCards.pitch: Chrome Enterprise Premium lets contractors work from any device — their own laptop, a shared machine, anything with Chrome — without ever touching your network or installing agents. They get access to exactly what they need, and you get full visibility and one-click revocation when the engagement ends.
keyPoints: No device management required — works on personal or unmanaged devices
Access scoped to approved apps and sites only
Full audit trail of what they accessed and when
Revoke access instantly — no IT ticket needed

pitchCards.title: Policy Enforcement Without MDM
pitchCards.pitch: Most MDM tools require you to enrol the device — and employees push back. Chrome Enterprise Premium enforces your security policies at the browser level, not the device level. That means you can control what employees do at work, without touching anything personal.
keyPoints: No MDM enrolment needed — policies live in the browser
Prevent downloads, copy-paste, screenshots in work context
Works on any OS — Windows, Mac, Chromebook, personal phones
Employees keep full control of their personal apps and data

pitchCards.title: Stop Data Leaving Through the Browser
pitchCards.pitch: The browser is where most work happens — and most leaks. Chrome Enterprise Premium gives you DLP controls at the point of use: prevent downloads to unmanaged devices, block copy-paste to personal apps, watermark sensitive content, and get alerts when someone tries to exfiltrate data.
keyPoints: Block file downloads to unmanaged or personal devices
Restrict copy-paste between work and personal contexts
Watermark sensitive pages and documents
Real-time alerts on risky data movement

pitchCards.title: Govern AI Tool Usage Across Your Organisation
pitchCards.pitch: You can't stop employees using AI tools — but you can make sure they're using approved ones, and that sensitive data isn't flowing into public models. Chrome Enterprise Premium lets you allow or block specific AI tools and control what content employees can paste into them.
keyPoints: Allow approved AI tools, block consumer/public alternatives
Prevent pasting sensitive data into AI prompts
URL filtering covers new AI tools as they emerge
Audit log of AI tool access per user

pitchCards.title: Phishing and Web Threat Protection on Any Device
pitchCards.pitch: Traditional web filtering only covers managed, on-network devices. Chrome Enterprise Premium extends protection to every Chrome session — personal devices, home networks, field workers — so you're not leaving gaps where the most dangerous clicks happen.
keyPoints: Real-time phishing and malware URL blocking
Works off-network and on personal devices
Custom allow/block lists for your industry requirements
No agent or device enrolment required

pitchCards.title: Windows Apps via Chrome — Without the Infrastructure
pitchCards.pitch: Cameyo streams Windows applications directly into the Chrome browser — no VDI infrastructure, no Citrix licences, no per-device agents. Users get a native-feeling app experience from any device with Chrome, and IT manages everything from a single web console.
keyPoints: Any Windows app streamed to any device with Chrome
Eliminate Citrix/RDS infrastructure and licence costs
Deploy new apps in hours, not weeks
Pairs with CEP for a fully managed, secure browser workspace
